questions.question: What key finding did the cMIND study (2026) reveal about the relationship between MIND diet adherence and cognitive protection?

options.text: Only perfect adherence provides any cognitive benefit
The relationship is nonlinear — the greatest benefit comes from moving from low to moderate adherence
Higher adherence actually increased cognitive impairment risk
The MIND diet only benefits people under age 60

questions.explanation: The cMIND study found a nonlinear relationship between diet adherence and cognitive protection — the greatest benefit came from moving from low to moderate adherence. This means you do not need perfect adherence to benefit; even modest dietary improvements provide meaningful cognitive protection.

questions.question: Why are standard serum B12 blood tests potentially unreliable for older adults?

options.text: B12 tests are not available for patients over 65
Serum B12 levels can appear normal even when functional B12 status is inadequate, because B12 may be bound to inactive carriers
Older adults' blood is too thick for accurate B12 measurement
B12 is not important for brain function in older adults

questions.explanation: Standard serum B12 levels can appear normal even when functional B12 status is inadequate, because a significant portion of serum B12 may be bound to haptocorrin (an inactive carrier) rather than transcobalamin (the active carrier). More reliable markers include methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine levels.

questions.question: Which common class of medications significantly impairs absorption of B12, magnesium, calcium, and iron?

options.text: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors)
Antibiotics
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)
Blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors)

questions.explanation: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), widely prescribed acid-reducing medications, significantly impair absorption of vitamin B12, magnesium, calcium, and iron — all critical for brain function. Long-term PPI use has also been independently associated with increased risk of dementia.

questions.question: What does the Mini Nutritional Assessment - Short Form (MNA-SF) evaluate, and what score indicates risk of malnutrition?

options.text: It measures depression severity; scores of 8-11 indicate moderate depression
It assesses nutritional status through 6 items; scores of 8-11 indicate risk of malnutrition
It evaluates medication side effects; scores of 8-11 indicate dangerous interactions
It screens for eating disorders; scores of 8-11 indicate active disordered eating

questions.explanation: The MNA-SF is a validated six-item screening tool that assesses nutritional status in older adults — including food intake decline, weight loss, mobility, psychological stress, and neuropsychological problems. Scores of 0-7 indicate malnutrition, 8-11 indicate risk of malnutrition, and 12-14 indicate normal nutritional status.

questions.question: In the US MIND Diet study (2024), which population showed a stronger risk reduction for cognitive decline?

options.text: Men showed a 10% greater risk reduction than women
Adults under 60 showed the strongest benefit
Women showed a stronger 6% risk reduction compared to 4% overall
There was no difference between any subgroups

questions.explanation: The 2024 US MIND study found a 4% overall reduced risk of cognitive decline, with a stronger 6% risk reduction observed in women specifically. This gender difference may relate to hormonal changes during menopause that affect brain metabolism, where the MIND diet's anti-inflammatory foods may partially compensate.

title: Older Adults: MIND Diet, Polypharmacy, and Brain Health - Quiz
